meta.name: Vector Database
meta.description: Similarity search engine with vector storage, distance metrics, brute-force baseline, HNSW approximate nearest neighbor index, vector quantization, and a query API.
meta.essence: Multi-dimensional vector storage with graph-based approximate nearest neighbor search using hierarchical navigable small world graphs (HNSW), distance metric computations (cosine distance, L2, dot product), vector quantization for memory efficiency, and metadata filtering—trading exact accuracy for sub-linear query time complexity.
meta.why_important: Vector similarity search is fundamental to modern AI applications including semantic search, recommendation systems, and RAG pipelines. Building a vector database from scratch teaches core data structures (graphs, quantization), systems programming (memory management, SIMD optimization), and performance engineering critical for infrastructure roles.
learning_outcomes: Design memory-aligned contiguous vector storage with efficient batch I/O
Implement distance metrics (cosine distance, L2, dot product) with SIMD optimization
Build exact brute-force KNN as a correctness baseline for measuring ANN recall
Implement HNSW graph construction and search with configurable M, efConstruction, and efSearch parameters
Implement scalar and product quantization for memory-efficient large-scale search
Build a query API with metadata filtering, batch operations, and concurrent access
Measure and optimize recall@k, query latency, and memory usage under load

resources.name: Product Quantization for Nearest Neighbor Search (Jégou et al.)

milestones.name: Vector Storage Engine
milestones.description: Build efficient contiguous storage for fixed-dimension vectors with metadata, supporting batch operations, persistence, and compaction.
acceptance_criteria: Vectors are stored in contiguous memory with fixed dimensionality configured at collection creation time
Storage is aligned to 32-byte (AVX2) or 64-byte (AVX-512) boundaries for SIMD compatibility
Batch insert adds N vectors in a single operation, measured to be at least 5x faster than N individual inserts for N=1000
Vector retrieval by ID returns the vector and associated metadata in O(1) via an ID-to-offset index
Deletion marks vectors as tombstoned; storage compaction reclaims space by rewriting live vectors contiguously
Memory-mapped file storage enables efficient access to datasets larger than available RAM
Persistence survives process restart—stored vectors are recoverable from disk
pitfalls: Not aligning vectors to cache line or SIMD boundaries kills distance computation performance by 2-4x
Memory mapping without proper error handling on disk full causes silent corruption
Dynamic allocation per vector (e.g., individual heap allocations) fragments memory and destroys cache locality
Partial writes during serialization corrupt the storage file—use atomic rename on fsync'd temp file
Tombstone accumulation without compaction wastes space and slows sequential scans
concepts: Memory alignment for SIMD operations (AVX2 needs 32-byte, AVX-512 needs 64-byte)
Contiguous storage layout for cache-efficient sequential access
Memory-mapped file lifecycle (mmap, msync, munmap)
Tombstone-based deletion with background compaction
deliverables: Fixed-dimension vector storage engine with configurable dimensionality
SIMD-aligned contiguous memory layout
Vector ID-to-offset index for O(1) retrieval
Batch insert operation for bulk loading
Tombstone deletion with compaction process
Memory-mapped file persistence with crash-safe writes

milestones.name: Distance Metrics
milestones.description: Implement distance/similarity functions with SIMD optimization and establish clear conventions for similarity vs distance ordering.
acceptance_criteria: Cosine distance computed as 1.0 - (dot(a,b) / (norm(a) * norm(b))), returning values in [0, 2] where 0 means identical
Euclidean (L2) distance computed as sqrt(sum((a_i - b_i)^2)), returning non-negative values
Dot product (inner product) similarity computed correctly; note that higher values mean more similar (ordering is inverted vs distance)
All metrics produce results within 1e-6 of a reference implementation for a test suite of at least 100 vector pairs
SIMD-optimized implementations are at least 3x faster than naive scalar loops for 768-dimensional vectors (measured via benchmark)
Pre-normalized vector detection skips redundant normalization for cosine distance, verified to produce identical results
Batch distance computation (1 query vs N vectors) processes at least 1M 128-dim comparisons per second
pitfalls: Confusing similarity (higher=better) with distance (lower=better) causes inverted rankings—establish a convention and enforce it everywhere
Not normalizing vectors before cosine computation produces incorrect results; alternatively use cosine distance formula that handles unnormalized inputs
Float accumulation in high dimensions loses precision; use compensated summation (Kahan) for >512 dimensions
Comparing distances from different metrics directly (e.g., L2 vs cosine) is mathematically meaningless
SIMD intrinsics are platform-specific; provide scalar fallback for portability
concepts: Cosine distance vs cosine similarity (1 - similarity = distance)
SIMD vectorization (AVX2, NEON) for parallel float arithmetic
Numerical stability in floating-point accumulation

concepts.Distance metric properties: symmetry, non-negativity, triangle inequality (L2 and cosine distance satisfy these; dot product does not)

deliverables: Cosine distance computation (1 - cosine_similarity)
Euclidean L2 distance computation
Dot product similarity computation
SIMD-optimized implementations with scalar fallback
Batch distance computation (1-vs-N)
Pre-normalized vector fast path
Benchmark suite comparing SIMD vs scalar performance

milestones.name: Brute Force KNN (Baseline)
milestones.description: Implement exact K-nearest neighbor search as a correctness baseline for measuring HNSW recall in subsequent milestones.
acceptance_criteria: Exact KNN returns the true top-k nearest vectors by computing distance to every stored vector
Top-k selection uses a max-heap of size k, achieving O(N log k) complexity instead of O(N log N) full sort
Metadata filtering (pre-filter) restricts candidates before distance computation based on field predicates
Batch query support executes M queries returning results equivalent to M individual queries
Performance baseline is established: query latency measured at 10K, 100K, and 1M vectors for k=10
Ground truth export generates a file mapping query vectors to their exact top-k results for recall measurement
pitfalls: Using full sort instead of heap-based top-k selection is O(N log N) instead of O(N log k)—significant for large k
Scanning vectors in non-sequential memory order destroys cache performance; iterate contiguously
Pre-filtering that evaluates metadata predicates *after* distance computation wastes CPU; filter first when selectivity is high
Copying vectors into result arrays instead of returning indices wastes memory
concepts: Heap-based top-k selection maintains a bounded result set during linear scan
Cache-efficient sequential scanning maximizes memory bandwidth
Pre-filtering vs post-filtering tradeoff for metadata predicates
Ground truth generation for recall@k measurement
deliverables: Exact KNN search with max-heap top-k selection
Metadata pre-filtering restricting candidates by field predicates
Batch query execution for multiple queries
Performance benchmark at 10K, 100K, 1M scale
Ground truth export for recall measurement
Threshold-based search returning all vectors within a distance cutoff

milestones.name: HNSW Index
milestones.description: Implement the Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) graph for approximate nearest neighbor search with sub-linear query complexity.
acceptance_criteria: HNSW graph construction assigns layers using the probabilistic formula: level = floor(-ln(uniform_random()) * mL) where mL = 1/ln(M)
Configurable parameters: M (max connections per node, default 16), efConstruction (build-time exploration width, default 200), efSearch (query-time exploration width, default 50)
Graph construction maintains bidirectional edges and enforces maximum M connections per node via neighbor selection heuristic
Greedy search traverses from entry point at top layer, descending to base layer, then expanding with efSearch candidates
Incremental insert adds new vectors without full index rebuild, maintaining graph connectivity invariants
Recall@10 ≥ 0.95 on a 100K vector dataset when efSearch=100, measured against brute-force ground truth from M3
Query latency is at least 10x faster than brute-force on 100K vectors for k=10 (measured via benchmark)
Index serialization persists the full graph structure to disk; deserialization restores identical search behavior
pitfalls: Not maintaining bidirectional edges during insertion causes unreachable nodes and recall collapse
Not limiting max connections per node (M_max = M for base layer, 2*M for upper layers per original paper) causes memory bloat
Greedy search without backtracking (using a priority queue of candidates) gets stuck in local optima
Neighbor selection heuristic must prioritize diversity (not just proximity) to maintain navigable small world properties
efSearch too low gives poor recall; too high degrades to near-brute-force latency. Tune empirically.
concepts: Probabilistic skip list-like layer structure for hierarchical navigation
Greedy best-first search with candidate priority queue and backtracking
Neighbor selection heuristic balancing proximity and diversity
Layer probability distribution controls graph height and connectivity
Recall@k measures fraction of true nearest neighbors found by ANN
deliverables: Multi-layer graph structure with probabilistic level assignment
Node insertion with bidirectional edge maintenance and neighbor pruning
Greedy search with layer descent and efSearch-controlled base layer exploration
Configurable M, efConstruction, and efSearch parameters
Recall@k measurement against brute-force ground truth
Index serialization and deserialization
Benchmark comparing HNSW vs brute-force latency and recall

milestones.name: Vector Quantization
milestones.description: Implement scalar quantization (SQ) and product quantization (PQ) for memory-efficient approximate distance computation, enabling large-scale search that would not fit in RAM with full-precision vectors.
acceptance_criteria: Scalar quantization (SQ8) maps float32 values to uint8 using per-dimension min/max scaling, reducing memory by 4x
Product quantization (PQ) splits vectors into M subspaces, quantizes each to one of 256 centroids (trained via k-means), and stores M uint8 codes per vector
Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC) computes approximate distances using precomputed lookup tables, avoiding full vector decompression
SQ8 recall@10 ≥ 0.90 on the same 100K dataset used in M3/M4 (measured against brute-force ground truth)
PQ with M=8 subspaces achieves at least 16x memory reduction compared to float32 storage (measured)
Quantization can be combined with HNSW—HNSW graph uses quantized distance for graph traversal, then re-ranks top candidates with exact distance
Training (codebook generation for PQ) completes in under 60 seconds for 100K training vectors
pitfalls: Per-dimension min/max for scalar quantization must be computed from training data, not hardcoded—data distribution varies
PQ codebook training with too few iterations of k-means produces poor centroids and low recall
Asymmetric distance computation requires precomputing M×256 lookup table per query—this amortizes only with large candidate sets
Combining quantized distance with HNSW requires careful threshold tuning—quantization errors can cause HNSW to skip true nearest neighbors
Quantization error is higher for vectors with high variance across dimensions; monitor per-dimension error distribution
concepts: Scalar quantization maps continuous values to discrete integer levels
Product quantization decomposes high-dimensional space into lower-dimensional subspaces
Codebook training uses k-means clustering to learn representative centroids per subspace
Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC) uses lookup tables for fast approximate distance

concepts.Two-phase search: quantized recall candidates, then exact re-ranking of top candidates

deliverables: Scalar quantization (SQ8) with per-dimension min/max calibration
Product quantization with configurable subspace count and codebook training
Asymmetric distance computation using precomputed lookup tables
HNSW + PQ integration for memory-efficient approximate search
Recall and memory reduction measurements comparing FP32, SQ8, and PQ
Benchmark suite measuring recall, latency, and memory usage across configurations

milestones.name: Query API & Server
milestones.description: Expose vector operations via a REST or gRPC API with concurrent access, metadata filtering, and batch operations.
acceptance_criteria: REST or gRPC API supports insert, upsert, search, and delete operations with OpenAPI or proto schema
Search API accepts query vector, k, distance metric, optional metadata filter predicates, and optional efSearch override
Metadata filtering during ANN search supports pre-filtering (filter before search) and post-filtering (filter after search) modes with configurable strategy
Batch operations process multiple inserts or queries in a single request, measured to reduce per-operation latency by at least 50% vs individual calls
Concurrent reads and writes are handled safely via read-write locks or lock-free structures; verified by concurrent stress test with no data corruption
Collection management API supports create (with dimension, metric, and index config), list, and delete operations
Query timeout terminates long-running searches after a configurable deadline
pitfalls: Blocking all reads during index updates causes availability gaps—use copy-on-write or RW-lock with read preference
Not setting query timeouts allows runaway searches to consume resources indefinitely
Pre-filtering with very selective predicates can reduce the candidate set so much that recall drops—document this tradeoff
Deserializing entire request body before validation wastes memory on malformed requests—validate incrementally
Missing API versioning makes backward-incompatible changes dangerous
concepts: Read-write locking for concurrent vector access
Pre-filtering vs post-filtering tradeoff for metadata-constrained ANN
gRPC streaming for efficient batch operations
Backpressure handling for high query loads
API versioning for forward compatibility
deliverables: Insert/upsert API accepting vectors with metadata
Search API with k, metric, filter, and efSearch parameters
Delete API removing vectors by ID
Collection management (create, list, delete)
Pre-filter and post-filter modes for metadata-constrained search
Batch insert and batch query endpoints
Concurrent stress test verifying data integrity under load

blueprint.title: Vector Database
blueprint.overview:
A vector database is a specialized storage engine designed for similarity search over high-dimensional vector embeddings—the dense numerical representations that power modern AI applications like semantic search, recommendation systems, and RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines. Unlike traditional databases that excel at exact matches and range queries, vector databases are optimized for finding the 'nearest' vectors in multi-dimensional space, where 'nearness' is defined by distance metrics like cosine similarity or Euclidean distance.

The core challenge of vector search is the curse of dimensionality: as dimensions increase, traditional index structures (B-trees, hash indexes) become ineffective, and even distance computations become expensive. This project tackles that challenge head-on by building a complete vector database from scratch, including memory-aligned vector storage, SIMD-optimized distance metrics, exact KNN as a baseline, the HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) graph algorithm for sub-linear approximate search, and vector quantization for memory efficiency.

By implementing these components, you'll understand why vector databases are designed the way they are—not as a black box, but as an emergent solution to fundamental mathematical and systems constraints. You'll see how approximate algorithms trade accuracy for speed, how memory layout affects performance, and how quantization enables billion-scale search on commodity hardware.
blueprint.design_philosophy: This project teaches the fundamental tension between accuracy and efficiency in similarity search. Every design decision—HNSW parameters, quantization methods, memory layout—represents a point on the accuracy-speed-memory tradeoff curve. By building from primitives (distance metrics) to complex structures (HNSW graphs) to production systems (query APIs), learners see how each layer builds on the previous and why production vector databases make the choices they do. The progression from brute-force (correct but slow) to HNSW (approximate but fast) makes the approximation explicit and measurable through recall metrics.

implementation.rationale: Vector databases demand three things: (1) SIMD optimization for distance computation—Rust's portable_simd and aligned memory types make this ergonomic, (2) memory safety without garbage collection for cache-predictable performance, and (3) fearless concurrency for parallel batch operations. Rust's zero-cost abstractions and borrow checker provide memory safety guarantees critical for low-level storage code while maintaining C-level performance. The strong type system also helps encode invariants (e.g., normalized vs unnormalized vectors) at compile time.
implementation.style_guide: Use snake_case for functions/variables, PascalCase for types. Prefer Result<T, E> with custom error types over unwrap(). Use #[repr(C, align(64))] for SIMD-aligned structures. Document safety requirements for unsafe blocks. Prefer iterators over explicit loops where idiomatic. Use cargo fmt and clippy.
implementation.build_system: Cargo (cargo build, cargo test, cargo bench)

alternatives.tradeoff: Maximum control and mature SIMD intrinsics, but manual memory management increases bug risk in complex graph algorithms
Simpler concurrency model, but garbage collection pauses are problematic for latency-sensitive search and SIMD support is limited
Rapid prototyping with NumPy, but cannot achieve production-level performance without C extensions—defeats the 'build from scratch' goal

assumed_known: Dot product and vector norms from linear algebra
Basic file I/O and memory-mapped files concept

must_teach_first.concept: Curse of dimensionality in nearest neighbor search

milestones.title: Vector Storage Engine
milestones.summary: Build the foundational storage layer: contiguous, SIMD-aligned memory for fixed-dimension vectors with metadata, batch operations, persistence via memory-mapped files, and tombstone-based deletion with compaction.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think storing vectors is like storing any other data—just serialize to JSON or put each vector in its own heap allocation. They assume the database will handle efficiency, or that modern computers are fast enough that memory layout doesn't matter.
milestones.reveal: Memory layout is THE critical factor for vector database performance. A single cache miss costs 100+ cycles. Non-contiguous storage (individual heap allocations per vector) causes 2-4x slowdown in distance computations because the CPU spends more time waiting for memory than doing math. Alignment to cache line (64 bytes) and SIMD register (32/64 bytes) boundaries is not optional—it's the difference between interactive and unusable. This is why production vector databases obsess over memory layout.
cascade: SIMD Distance Computation (M2) — Contiguous aligned storage enables loading 8 floats per instruction; scattered storage defeats SIMD entirely
Cache-efficient brute-force scan (M3) — Sequential memory access achieves 50+ GB/s bandwidth; random access drops to <1 GB/s
HNSW neighbor storage (M4) — Graph edges must also be contiguous for cache-efficient traversal; pointer-chasing kills performance
Quantization memory savings (M5) — Understanding raw storage size (4 bytes × dim × N vectors) makes quantization's 4-16x savings concrete
Operating system page cache (cross-domain) — mmap leverages the OS page cache automatically; understanding this explains why 'memory-mapped files' feel like memory
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Vectors are stored in contiguous memory with fixed dimensionality configured at collection creation time
Storage is aligned to 32-byte (AVX2) or 64-byte (AVX-512) boundaries for SIMD compatibility
Batch insert adds N vectors in a single operation, measured to be at least 5x faster than N individual inserts for N=1000
Vector retrieval by ID returns the vector and associated metadata in O(1) via an ID-to-offset index
Deletion marks vectors as tombstoned; storage compaction reclaims space by rewriting live vectors contiguously
Memory-mapped file storage enables efficient access to datasets larger than available RAM
Persistence survives process restart—stored vectors are recoverable from disk

milestones.title: Distance Metrics
milestones.summary: Implement the three core distance/similarity functions—cosine distance, Euclidean (L2) distance, and dot product—with SIMD optimization achieving 3x+ speedup over scalar code. Establish conventions for similarity (higher=better) vs distance (lower=better).
milestones.misconception: Developers often treat distance computation as trivial—just a loop with some math. They assume the compiler will optimize it, or that it doesn't matter because 'it's just O(d) where d is dimensionality.' They confuse similarity with distance, using them interchangeably.
milestones.reveal: Distance computation is the HOT LOOP of vector search—it runs millions or billions of times per query batch. A single distance call is fast, but multiplied by N vectors × Q queries, it dominates runtime. SIMD doesn't just make it 3x faster; it makes large-scale search economically viable. More subtly, the similarity vs distance convention matters: mixing them up inverts your rankings, returning the WORST results instead of the best. This happens in production systems more often than you'd think.
cascade: Brute-force KNN performance (M3) — Distance is the inner loop; SIMD optimization directly translates to queries/second
HNSW greedy search (M4) — Every graph traversal step requires distance computations; SIMD compounds across the search path
Quantization ADC lookup (M5) — Asymmetric distance computation is itself a distance metric variant; understanding the core metrics makes ADC intuitive
Information retrieval ranking (cross-domain) — Search engines use BM25 and TF-IDF; understanding 'higher=better' vs 'lower=better' convention applies everywhere
Numerical stability in ML (cross-domain) — Kahan summation and precision issues in high dimensions appear in neural network training too
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Cosine distance computed as 1.0 - (dot(a,b) / (norm(a) * norm(b))), returning values in [0, 2] where 0 means identical
Euclidean (L2) distance computed as sqrt(sum((a_i - b_i)^2)), returning non-negative values
Dot product (inner product) similarity computed correctly; note that higher values mean more similar (ordering is inverted vs distance)
All metrics produce results within 1e-6 of a reference implementation for a test suite of at least 100 vector pairs
SIMD-optimized implementations are at least 3x faster than naive scalar loops for 768-dimensional vectors (measured via benchmark)
Pre-normalized vector detection skips redundant normalization for cosine distance, verified to produce identical results
Batch distance computation (1 query vs N vectors) processes at least 1M 128-dim comparisons per second

milestones.title: Brute Force KNN (Baseline)
milestones.summary: Implement exact K-nearest neighbor search via linear scan with max-heap top-k selection. This establishes the ground truth for measuring recall in approximate algorithms and reveals why we need HNSW.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think brute-force search is obviously too slow and should be skipped. Or they implement it with a full sort (O(N log N)) instead of heap-based top-k (O(N log k)). They assume any 'real' system uses fancy indexes from the start.
milestones.reveal: Brute-force is the CORRECTNESS baseline—you cannot measure ANN quality without it. O(N log k) with a heap is significantly faster than sorting for small k. More importantly, brute-force at 100K vectors is still fast enough (milliseconds) to be useful; the scalability cliff happens around 1M+ vectors. Understanding WHERE brute-force breaks down and WHY is essential context for appreciating HNSW's design.
cascade: Recall@k measurement methodology (M4) — Ground truth from brute-force enables quantitative comparison of ANN algorithms
Heap data structure mastery (cross-domain) — Top-k with bounded heaps appears in streaming algorithms, priority schedulers, and leaderboards
Metadata filtering strategy (M6) — Pre-filtering vs post-filtering tradeoff is first visible here; same decision applies to HNSW
Cache-efficient sequential scanning (M1 connection) — Brute-force maximizes memory bandwidth; this is why contiguous storage matters
Algorithm selection in databases (cross-domain) — Every database chooses between index-accelerated and sequential scan based on selectivity; vector DBs are no different
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Exact KNN returns the true top-k nearest vectors by computing distance to every stored vector
Top-k selection uses a max-heap of size k, achieving O(N log k) complexity instead of O(N log N) full sort
Metadata filtering (pre-filter) restricts candidates before distance computation based on field predicates
Batch query support executes M queries returning results equivalent to M individual queries
Performance baseline is established: query latency measured at 10K, 100K, and 1M vectors for k=10
Ground truth export generates a file mapping query vectors to their exact top-k results for recall measurement

milestones.title: HNSW Index
milestones.summary: Implement the Hierarchical Navigable Small World graph algorithm for approximate nearest neighbor search with sub-linear complexity. This is the core innovation that makes billion-scale vector search possible.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think HNSW is just 'a graph where you connect nearby vectors' or assume it's similar to k-NN graphs. They don't understand the hierarchical layer structure, the probabilistic level assignment, or why greedy search with backtracking is necessary.
milestones.reveal: HNSW's brilliance is combining TWO ideas: (1) skip list-like hierarchical layers for O(log N) routing to the right neighborhood, and (2) navigable small world graphs that balance short-range and long-range connections to avoid local optima. The layer structure means you zoom in from 'continent level' to 'city level' to 'street level' as you descend. Without hierarchy, you'd need to explore too many nodes; without NSW properties, greedy search would get stuck. The combination achieves sub-millisecond search on millions of vectors.
cascade: Vector quantization integration (M5) — HNSW graphs can use quantized distances for traversal; understanding HNSW structure makes this optimization clear
Skip lists and probabilistic data structures (cross-domain) — The layer probability formula is identical to skip lists; learn one, understand both
Graph database traversal (cross-domain) — Greedy search with priority queues is similar to Dijkstra/A*; graph algorithm fundamentals transfer
Approximation in other domains (cross-domain) — Count-Min Sketch, Bloom filters, and HNSW all trade exactness for efficiency; the pattern repeats
Parameter tuning in ML systems (cross-domain) — M, efConstruction, efSearch are hyperparameters; tuning methodology applies to neural network training
yaml_acceptance_criteria: HNSW graph construction assigns layers using the probabilistic formula: level = floor(-ln(uniform_random()) * mL) where mL = 1/ln(M)
Configurable parameters: M (max connections per node, default 16), efConstruction (build-time exploration width, default 200), efSearch (query-time exploration width, default 50)
Graph construction maintains bidirectional edges and enforces maximum M connections per node via neighbor selection heuristic
Greedy search traverses from entry point at top layer, descending to base layer, then expanding with efSearch candidates
Incremental insert adds new vectors without full index rebuild, maintaining graph connectivity invariants
Recall@10 ≥ 0.95 on a 100K vector dataset when efSearch=100, measured against brute-force ground truth from M3
Query latency is at least 10x faster than brute-force on 100K vectors for k=10 (measured via benchmark)
Index serialization persists the full graph structure to disk; deserialization restores identical search behavior

milestones.title: Vector Quantization
milestones.summary: Implement scalar quantization (SQ8) and product quantization (PQ) for memory-efficient approximate distance computation, enabling billion-scale search on commodity hardware.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think quantization is just 'compression' and assume it's lossy in unacceptable ways. Or they think it's too complex to implement. They don't understand asymmetric distance computation (ADC) and why it avoids decompression.
milestones.reveal: Quantization is not just compression—it's a fundamentally different way to represent and compute distances. The key insight of product quantization is that you don't need to decompress to compute distances; you precompute a lookup table from the query to all 256 centroids per subspace, then distance is just M table lookups and additions. This is 10-100x faster than full vector distance and enables searching vectors that don't fit in RAM. The recall loss (5-10%) is often acceptable for the memory savings (16-32x).
cascade: Large-scale deployment (M6) — Quantization enables billion-vector datasets on single machines; this is how production systems scale
JPEG and MP3 compression (cross-domain) — Lossy compression trades quality for size; PQ makes this tradeoff explicit and measurable via recall
Hash tables and fingerprints (cross-domain) — Quantization codes are like learned hashes; similar vectors have similar codes
Neural network quantization (cross-domain) — INT8/INT4 quantization in deep learning uses the same min/max scaling principles
K-means clustering fundamentals (cross-domain) — PQ codebook training IS k-means; understanding one enables understanding the other
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Scalar quantization (SQ8) maps float32 values to uint8 using per-dimension min/max scaling, reducing memory by 4x
Product quantization (PQ) splits vectors into M subspaces, quantizes each to one of 256 centroids (trained via k-means), and stores M uint8 codes per vector
Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC) computes approximate distances using precomputed lookup tables, avoiding full vector decompression
SQ8 recall@10 ≥ 0.90 on the same 100K dataset used in M3/M4 (measured against brute-force ground truth)
PQ with M=8 subspaces achieves at least 16x memory reduction compared to float32 storage (measured)
Quantization can be combined with HNSW—HNSW graph uses quantized distance for graph traversal, then re-ranks top candidates with exact distance
Training (codebook generation for PQ) completes in under 60 seconds for 100K training vectors

milestones.title: Query API & Server
milestones.summary: Expose vector operations via a REST or gRPC API with concurrent access control, metadata filtering, batch operations, and collection management—transforming the storage engine into a usable service.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think the API layer is trivial—just wrap function calls in HTTP endpoints. They underestimate concurrent access complexity, the importance of timeouts, and the subtlety of pre-filtering vs post-filtering for metadata.
milestones.reveal: The API layer is where correctness meets the real world. Concurrent reads during HNSW updates can corrupt the graph if not carefully managed. Query timeouts aren't optional—HNSW with high efSearch can run for seconds on large datasets. Pre-filtering with selective predicates can drop recall to near-zero because HNSW's routing may never reach the filtered region. These aren't edge cases; they're the dominant concerns in production.
cascade: Production vector databases (cross-domain) — Understanding this API design makes Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus API choices transparent
Database concurrency control (cross-domain) — Read-write locks, copy-on-write, and MVCC patterns apply to all databases
Distributed systems backpressure (cross-domain) — Query timeouts and batch size limits are load shedding mechanisms
API versioning and compatibility (cross-domain) — Backward-compatible API evolution is a universal software engineering challenge
Observability and monitoring (cross-domain) — Production APIs need latency percentiles, error rates, and recall metrics
yaml_acceptance_criteria: REST or gRPC API supports insert, upsert, search, and delete operations with OpenAPI or proto schema
Search API accepts query vector, k, distance metric, optional metadata filter predicates, and optional efSearch override
Metadata filtering during ANN search supports pre-filtering (filter before search) and post-filtering (filter after search) modes with configurable strategy
Batch operations process multiple inserts or queries in a single request, measured to reduce per-operation latency by at least 50% vs individual calls
Concurrent reads and writes are handled safely via read-write locks or lock-free structures; verified by concurrent stress test with no data corruption
Collection management API supports create (with dimension, metric, and index config), list, and delete operations
Query timeout terminates long-running searches after a configurable deadline

diagrams.title: Vector Database Architecture: Satellite Map
diagrams.description: System-wide view showing all six milestones as interconnected components. Vector Storage Engine at the foundation, feeding into Distance Metrics and Brute-Force KNN. HNSW Index sits above storage, using distance metrics. Vector Quantization compresses storage and accelerates HNSW. Query API wraps everything. Shows data flow from insert (storage → index) to query (API → index → distance → results). Each component links to its anchor ID.

diagrams.title: Contiguous Vector Storage Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Visual representation of memory layout: header block (dimension, count, alignment), followed by contiguous vector data blocks, each aligned to 64-byte boundary. Shows ID-to-offset index as separate structure. Contrasts with fragmented individual allocations. Indicates cache line boundaries and SIMD register widths.

diagrams.title: SIMD Alignment: Why 32/64 Bytes Matter
diagrams.description: Microscopic view of a single vector in memory, showing 64-byte alignment boundary. Demonstrates aligned vs misaligned load: aligned load is single instruction, misaligned requires two loads + merge. Shows AVX2 YMM register (256-bit = 32 bytes) and AVX-512 ZMM register (512-bit = 64 bytes) overlaying aligned data.

diagrams.title: Memory-Mapped File Lifecycle
diagrams.description: State diagram showing mmap lifecycle: OPEN → MMAP → READ/WRITE → MSYNC → MUNMAP → CLOSE. Shows virtual memory mapping to file pages, page fault handling, and dirty page writeback. Includes tombstone marking and compaction process rewriting live data.

diagrams.title: Tombstone Deletion and Compaction
diagrams.description: Before/after visualization: storage with scattered tombstones (deleted vectors marked X) vs post-compaction contiguous live vectors. Shows ID-to-offset index updates during compaction. Illustrates space reclamation.

diagrams.title: Batch Insert vs Individual Insert Performance
diagrams.description: Data walk comparing two paths: individual inserts (allocate, copy, update index × N) vs batch insert (single allocation, memcpy block, bulk index update). Shows where overhead comes from: allocation overhead, index updates, potential reallocation.

diagrams.title: Persistent Storage File Format
diagrams.description: Binary layout of the on-disk file: magic bytes, version, header (dimension, count, flags), vector data section, metadata section, ID-to-offset index. Shows atomic write pattern: write to temp file, fsync, rename.

diagrams.title: SIMD vs Scalar Distance Computation
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of scalar loop (4 floats per iteration, 4 operations each) vs SIMD (8 floats per iteration, 1 operation). Shows instruction count reduction and memory bandwidth utilization. Benchmark numbers: scalar ~50M ops/sec, SIMD ~200M ops/sec for 128-dim.

diagrams.title: Distance Metric Formulas Visualized
diagrams.description: Geometric visualization of three metrics in 2D/3D: cosine distance as angle between vectors, L2 as straight-line distance, dot product as projection. Shows formula derivation and value ranges. Emphasizes similarity (higher=better) vs distance (lower=better) convention.

diagrams.title: Batch Distance: 1-vs-N Computation
diagrams.description: Trace example showing query vector q against N stored vectors. Memory access pattern: q loaded once, each v_i loaded sequentially. SIMD processes 8 distances at once. Shows how contiguous storage enables prefetching and cache efficiency.

diagrams.title: Floating-Point Accumulation: Kahan Summation
diagrams.description: Microscopic view of precision loss in high-dimensional dot product. Standard accumulation loses low bits to rounding. Kahan summation tracks and recovers lost precision. Shows error growth with dimension: 128-dim negligible, 768-dim noticeable, 1536+ significant.

diagrams.title: Pre-Normalized Vector Fast Path
diagrams.description: Decision flow: if vectors pre-normalized (norm=1), skip division in cosine distance. Shows storage of normalization flag per vector. Computational savings: dot product only, no sqrt or division.

diagrams.title: Max-Heap Top-K Selection
diagrams.description: Data structure visualization: max-heap of size k during linear scan. Shows insertion when distance < heap max, eviction of max. Final heap contains k smallest. Complexity comparison: O(N log k) vs O(N log N) for full sort. Example with N=1000, k=10.

diagrams.title: Brute-Force Linear Scan Flow
diagrams.description: Data walk through linear scan: iterate over contiguous vectors, compute distance, update heap if candidate. Shows memory access pattern (sequential) and cache utilization. Contrast with random access pattern of naive indexing.

diagrams.title: Pre-Filtering vs Post-Filtering Tradeoff
diagrams.description: Before/after comparison: pre-filter evaluates metadata predicate first (reduces candidates, may miss nearest if filter is wrong), post-filter computes all distances then filters (guaranteed correctness, wasted compute). Decision tree based on filter selectivity.

diagrams.title: Brute-Force Scalability: Where It Breaks
diagrams.description: Performance graph: latency vs N for k=10. Shows linear growth, with acceptable latency (<10ms) up to ~100K vectors, degradation at 1M (~100ms), unusable at 10M+. This is WHY we need HNSW.

diagrams.title: Ground Truth Export for Recall Measurement
diagrams.description: Process flow: generate query vectors, run brute-force KNN, export (query_id, [true_top_k_ids]) to file. This file becomes input to recall measurement in M4. Format specification.

diagrams.title: HNSW Hierarchical Layer Structure
diagrams.description: 3D visualization of multi-layer graph: top layer (few nodes, long-range connections), middle layers, base layer (all nodes, dense local connections). Shows entry point at top, greedy descent path. Analogy to skip list levels.

diagrams.title: Probabilistic Level Assignment
diagrams.description: Formula visualization: level = floor(-ln(uniform()) * mL) where mL = 1/ln(M). Shows probability distribution: P(level=0) ~ 63%, P(level=1) ~ 23%, etc. Histogram of expected node counts per level for M=16.

diagrams.title: Greedy Search with Layer Descent
diagrams.description: Step-by-step trace: start at entry point top layer, greedily move to closest neighbor at each layer, descend to next layer, repeat until base layer. At base layer, expand to efSearch candidates. Shows priority queue management.

diagrams.title: Neighbor Selection Heuristic
diagrams.description: Visualization of neighbor pruning: when node has >M candidates, select M that maximize diversity (not just proximity). Shows simple closest-k vs heuristic algorithm. Impact on recall and graph navigability.

diagrams.title: Node Insertion: Bidirectional Edge Maintenance
diagrams.description: Data walk for inserting new node: assign level, search for nearest at each level, connect bidirectional edges, prune neighbors exceeding M. Shows invariant maintenance: every edge has reverse edge.

diagrams.title: efSearch vs Recall vs Latency Tradeoff
diagrams.description: Three-axis graph: efSearch (x), recall@10 (y1), latency (y2). Shows optimal operating point. efSearch too low → poor recall; too high → approaches brute-force latency. Parameter tuning guide.

diagrams.title: Recall@k Calculation
diagrams.description: Formula and example: recall@k = |ANN_results ∩ ground_truth| / k. Shows Venn diagram of ANN results vs true top-k. Example: 8 overlap out of 10 → recall@10 = 0.80.

diagrams.title: HNSW Index Serialization Format
diagrams.description: Binary layout: header (M, efConstruction, max level), per-layer node lists, edge adjacency lists. Shows deserialization restoring identical graph structure. Incremental serialization for large indexes.

diagrams.title: HNSW vs Brute-Force: Latency Comparison
diagrams.description: Benchmark results at 10K, 100K, 1M vectors. Shows HNSW latency nearly constant (log N), brute-force linear. Recall overlay: HNSW at 0.95 recall vs brute-force at 1.0. The tradeoff made concrete.

diagrams.title: Scalar Quantization (SQ8): Float32 → UInt8
diagrams.description: Visualization of mapping: [min, max] range → [0, 255]. Formula: code = (value - min) / (max - min) * 255. Decompression: value ≈ min + code / 255 * (max - min). Shows precision loss (4 bytes → 1 byte = 4x savings).

diagrams.title: Product Quantization: Subspace Decomposition
diagrams.description: Vector split visualization: 128-dim vector → 8 subspaces of 16-dim each. Each subspace has 256 centroids (codebook). Vector encoded as 8 uint8 codes. Memory: 128×4=512 bytes → 8 bytes = 64x savings.

diagrams.title: PQ Codebook Training via K-Means
diagrams.description: Process flow: sample training vectors, split into subspaces, run k-means (k=256) per subspace, store 256 centroids × M subspaces. Shows training time scaling with sample size. Centroid visualization in 2D.

diagrams.title: Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC)
diagrams.description: The key insight: query vector NOT quantized. Precompute distance from query to all 256 centroids per subspace → M×256 lookup table. Distance = sum of table[code[i]] for i in 0..M-1. No decompression needed!

diagrams.title: HNSW + PQ: Two-Phase Search
diagrams.description: Search flow: HNSW traversal uses PQ distance (fast, approximate), collect top candidates, re-rank with exact distance. Shows recall recovery from quantization error. Parameter: re-rank factor (e.g., retrieve 100, re-rank top 10).

diagrams.title: Memory Usage: FP32 vs SQ8 vs PQ
diagrams.description: Bar chart comparison for 1M vectors, 768-dim: FP32 = 3GB, SQ8 = 750MB (4x), PQ (M=48) = 48MB (64x). Shows memory-accuracy tradeoff with recall numbers overlay.

diagrams.title: Query API Architecture
diagrams.description: REST/gRPC endpoint map: POST /collections, GET /collections, DELETE /collections/{id}, POST /vectors, POST /vectors/search, DELETE /vectors/{id}. Shows request/response flow through storage, index, and distance layers.

diagrams.title: Search Request Processing Flow
diagrams.description: Trace example: request validation → collection lookup → filter strategy selection (pre/post) → HNSW search → re-ranking (if quantized) → result assembly. Shows timeout check points.

diagrams.title: Concurrent Access: Read-Write Lock Model
diagrams.description: State diagram: readers acquire shared lock (multiple concurrent), writer acquires exclusive lock (blocks all). Shows copy-on-write alternative for HNSW updates. Deadlock prevention strategy.

diagrams.title: Batch Operation Efficiency
diagrams.description: Comparison: individual requests (N round trips, N serialization, N lock acquisitions) vs batch (1 round trip, bulk serialization, 1 lock acquisition). Shows 50%+ latency reduction mechanism.

diagrams.title: Pre-Filter vs Post-Filter for ANN
diagrams.description: Decision tree: low selectivity (many matches) → post-filter, high selectivity (few matches) → pre-filter. Shows recall impact: pre-filter may miss true nearest if they're in filtered-out region. Tradeoff visualization.

diagrams.title: Query Timeout Mechanism
diagrams.description: Implementation: deadline passed to search function, checked at each layer descent or candidate expansion. Shows early termination behavior and partial result handling.

diagrams.title: Collection Lifecycle Management
diagrams.description: State machine: CREATING (index init) → ACTIVE → DELETING → DELETED. Shows config stored at creation (dimension, metric, M, efConstruction). Index building for bulk insert.

diagrams.title: Concurrent Stress Test Scenario
diagrams.description: Test visualization: 10 writers + 100 readers for 60 seconds. Invariants checked: no corrupted distances, no orphaned nodes, recall remains stable. Shows read-write lock behavior under load.

tdd_blueprint.design_vision: A production-grade vector similarity search engine implementing HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) graphs for sub-linear approximate nearest neighbor search. The system trades exact accuracy for query speed through probabilistic graph traversal, achieving 10x+ latency improvement over brute-force while maintaining ≥95% recall. Core design principles: memory-aligned contiguous storage for SIMD compatibility, quantization for billion-scale memory efficiency, and concurrent-safe access patterns for production workloads.

modules.name: Vector Storage Engine
modules.description: Builds the foundational storage layer with SIMD-aligned contiguous memory layout for vectors. Handles persistence via memory-mapped files, tombstone-based deletion with background compaction, and O(1) retrieval via ID-to-offset indexing. Does NOT implement distance computation or search algorithms.

specs.inputs: Vector data (Vec<f32>), metadata (HashMap<String, MetadataValue>), dimension configuration
specs.outputs: AlignedVectorBuffer with contiguous f32 data, VectorStorage with slot management, serialized file format
specs.abstractions: AlignedVectorBuffer (memory-aligned allocation), VectorStorage (slot/index management), MmapVectorStorage (large dataset access)
specs.concurrency_model: RwLock-based: multiple concurrent readers OR single exclusive writer. Collection-level lock granularity.
performance_targets: Batch insert ≥5x faster than N individual inserts for N=1000
O(1) retrieval via hash map ID-to-offset
Crash-safe serialization via atomic rename pattern

implementation_phases.name: VectorStorage with slot allocation and ID mapping
Tombstone deletion and free list management

diagrams.title: Contiguous Vector Storage Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Shows aligned buffer layout with vectors stored sequentially, padding for 64-byte alignment, and offset calculation for O(1) access

diagrams.title: VectorStorage Architecture
diagrams.description: Module structure showing AlignedVectorBuffer, slots vector, id_to_offset HashMap, free_list, and their relationships

diagrams.title: SIMD Alignment Detail: 32/64 Byte Boundaries
diagrams.description: Visual explanation of why AVX2 needs 32-byte and AVX-512 needs 64-byte alignment, showing cache line boundaries and misalignment penalties

diagrams.title: Batch Insert vs Individual Insert Flow
diagrams.description: Data flow comparison showing single capacity check + sequential writes (batch) vs N lock acquisitions + scattered writes (individual)

diagrams.title: Tombstone Deletion State Machine
diagrams.description: States: Active → Tombstoned → Free (after compaction). Shows transitions and what happens to id_to_offset and free_list

diagrams.title: Compaction Algorithm Steps
diagrams.description: Before/after visualization showing tombstone gaps, old_to_new index mapping, and buffer rewrite

diagrams.title: Persistent Storage File Format
diagrams.description: Byte layout: FileHeader (64 bytes), vector data section, id_map section, slots section, metadata section with lengths

diagrams.title: Memory-Mapped File Lifecycle
diagrams.description: Sequence: open → mmap → access (page fault → OS loads) → msync → munmap. Shows interaction with OS page cache

diagrams.title: Crash-Safe Write Pattern
diagrams.description: Algorithm steps: write to temp file → fsync → atomic rename. Shows how this prevents partial-write corruption

diagrams.title: Generation Counter ABA Prevention
diagrams.description: Shows how generation counters prevent stale references when slot indices are reused after deletion/compaction

modules.name: Distance Metrics
modules.description: Implements SIMD-optimized distance/similarity functions (cosine, L2, dot product) with clear conventions for similarity (higher=better) vs distance (lower=better). Provides scalar reference implementations for correctness verification and AVX2 intrinsics for performance.

specs.inputs: Two vector slices &[f32], metric type enum
specs.outputs: Distance/similarity score as f32, Metric trait object for runtime dispatch
specs.abstractions: Metric trait (distance, is_similarity, name), NormalizedVector (pre-computed unit vectors), DistanceType enum
error_categories: Division by zero for zero vectors (handled gracefully)
specs.concurrency_model: Stateless distance functions are inherently thread-safe. Metric trait objects are Send + Sync.
performance_targets: SIMD implementations ≥3x faster than scalar for 768-dim vectors
All results within 1e-6 of scalar reference

implementation_phases.name: Kahan compensated summation for high dimensions

diagrams.title: Distance Metric Formulas Visualized
diagrams.description: Mathematical formulas for dot product, L2 distance, cosine similarity/distance with geometric interpretation in 2D/3D space

diagrams.title: SIMD vs Scalar Distance Computation
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison: scalar loop (1 mul + 1 add per iteration) vs AVX2 (8 muls + 8 adds per iteration with _mm256_fmadd_ps)

diagrams.title: AVX2 Horizontal Sum Reduction
diagrams.description: Step-by-step: __m256 → extract hi/lo 128-bit lanes → add lanes → horizontal add within 128-bit → final scalar extraction

diagrams.title: Floating-Point Accumulation Error
diagrams.description: Visualization of Kahan summation: shows how compensation term captures lost precision, comparing scalar vs Kahan error accumulation over 768 products

diagrams.title: Pre-Normalized Vector Fast Path
diagrams.description: Decision tree: detect unit vectors (norm ≈ 1.0) → skip norm computation in cosine → use dot product directly

diagrams.title: Batch Distance: 1-vs-N Pattern
diagrams.description: Shows query preprocessing (compute query norm once), then sequential access through database vectors with cache-efficient iteration

diagrams.title: Metric Trait Hierarchy
diagrams.description: Trait definition with impls for DotProduct, Euclidean, Cosine. Shows is_similarity() return values and distance() signatures

diagrams.title: Similarity vs Distance Ordering Convention
diagrams.description: Table comparing metrics: type (similarity/distance), range, ordering (higher/lower = better), and implications for top-k sorting

diagrams.title: Runtime SIMD Dispatch Flow
diagrams.description: Sequence: check CPUID for AVX2 → if supported, use avx2:: functions → else fall back to optimized scalar

modules.name: Brute Force KNN (Baseline)
modules.description: Implements exact K-nearest neighbor search via exhaustive linear scan. Uses heap-based O(N log k) top-k selection instead of O(N log N) full sort. Provides ground truth generation for recall measurement and metadata pre-filtering.

specs.inputs: Query vector, k, optional filter predicate, storage reference
specs.outputs: Vec<SearchResult> sorted by distance/similarity, GroundTruth for recall measurement
specs.abstractions: TopKSelector (bounded heap), BruteForceSearch (linear scan), BatchSearch (multiple queries), GroundTruth (recall measurement)
error_categories: Empty result set (no vectors pass filter)
specs.concurrency_model: Read lock on storage during search. BatchSearch holds lock once for multiple queries.
performance_targets: Heap-based selection 5x+ faster than full sort at 100K

implementation_phases.name: TopKSelector with max-heap for distance metrics
TopKSelector with min-heap for similarity metrics

diagrams.title: Brute-Force Linear Scan Flow
diagrams.description: Data flow: iterate all vectors → compute distance → update heap → extract sorted results. Shows O(N) distance computations

diagrams.title: Max-Heap Top-K Selection
diagrams.description: Shows heap structure: root = worst candidate in top-k, eviction when better candidate found, final extraction and sorting

diagrams.title: O(N log k) vs O(N log N) Comparison
diagrams.description: Bar chart comparing operations: heap (3.3M comparisons for N=1M, k=10) vs sort (20M comparisons)

diagrams.title: Pre-Filtering vs Post-Filtering Tradeoff
diagrams.description: Decision flow: pre-filter saves computation but may not find k results; post-filter guarantees k but wastes work

diagrams.title: Batch Search Cache Efficiency
diagrams.description: Shows shared lock acquisition, vectors staying in L2/L3 cache across queries, sequential access pattern

diagrams.title: Ground Truth Export Format
diagrams.description: JSON structure: metric_name, k, queries array with (query_vector, [(id, distance), ...]) pairs

diagrams.title: Recall@k Calculation
diagrams.description: Set intersection visualization: exact_ids ∩ approximate_ids / k. Shows recall = 0.8 example

diagrams.title: Brute-Force Scalability Cliff
diagrams.description: Latency curve: 10K (~5ms), 100K (~50ms), 1M (~500ms), 10M (~5s). Shows where brute-force becomes impractical

diagrams.title: Threshold-Based Search Flow
diagrams.description: Alternative to top-k: compute all distances → filter by threshold → return all within cutoff

diagrams.title: FilterStrategy::Auto Decision Logic
diagrams.description: Algorithm: sample 100 vectors → estimate selectivity → if >50% pass use pre-filter else post-filter

modules.name: HNSW Index
modules.description: Implements Hierarchical Navigable Small World graph for sub-linear approximate nearest neighbor search. Uses probabilistic layer assignment (skip list formula), greedy search with backtracking via candidate queues, and bidirectional edge maintenance during insertion.

specs.inputs: Vector ID for insertion, query vector for search, efSearch parameter
specs.outputs: Vec<HNSWResult> with approximate top-k, serialized index file
specs.abstractions: HNSWNode (vector_id, max_layer, neighbors per layer), HNSWIndex (graph + entry point), HNSWConfig (M, efConstruction, efSearch, ml)
specs.concurrency_model: RwLock on nodes HashMap and entry_point. Read lock for search, write lock for insertion.

diagrams.title: HNSW Hierarchical Layer Structure
diagrams.description: 3D visualization: Layer 2 (few nodes, long edges), Layer 1 (more nodes), Layer 0 (all nodes, short edges). Shows entry point at top

diagrams.title: Probabilistic Level Assignment Distribution
diagrams.description: Bar chart: ~50% at layer 0, ~25% at layer 1, ~12.5% at layer 2. Shows exponential decay from formula level = floor(-ln(uniform) * mL)

diagrams.title: Greedy Search with Layer Descent
diagrams.description: Sequence: start at entry point top layer → greedy move to closest neighbor → descend to next layer → repeat → base layer search with efSearch

diagrams.title: Two-Heap Search Structure
diagrams.description: Shows min-heap (candidates to explore) and max-heap (results, worst at top). Termination when closest candidate > worst result

diagrams.title: Node Insertion Process
diagrams.description: Steps: assign layer → find entry point → search each layer for neighbors → connect bidirectionally → prune if exceeding M_max

diagrams.title: Bidirectional Edge Maintenance
diagrams.description: Shows new node A connecting to neighbors B, C, D and their neighbor lists being updated to include A

diagrams.title: Neighbor Selection Heuristic
diagrams.description: Comparison: simple (take M closest) vs heuristic (prefer diversity, skip if closer to existing neighbor than to query)

diagrams.title: efSearch vs Recall vs Latency Tradeoff
diagrams.description: Curves showing recall increases with efSearch (0.70→0.99) while latency also increases. Optimal region at efSearch=50-100

diagrams.title: HNSW Index Serialization Format
diagrams.description: JSON structure: config, nodes array (vector_id, max_layer, neighbors per layer), entry_point, max_layer, metric_name

diagrams.title: Recall@k Calculation Against Ground Truth
diagrams.description: Shows HNSW results compared to brute-force ground truth, intersection counting, recall formula

diagrams.title: HNSW vs Brute-Force Latency Comparison
diagrams.description: Bar chart at different scales: 10K (similar), 100K (HNSW 10x faster), 1M (HNSW 50x faster)

diagrams.title: Skip List Analogy for Layer Assignment
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of skip list levels and HNSW layers, showing identical probabilistic structure

modules.name: Vector Quantization
modules.description: Implements scalar quantization (SQ8) for 4x compression and product quantization (PQ) for 16-32x compression. Uses Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC) with lookup tables for fast approximate distances without decompression. Integrates with HNSW for memory-efficient traversal.

specs.inputs: Training vectors, M (subspace count), storage vectors
specs.outputs: Quantized codes (Vec<u8>), ADC lookup tables, approximate distances
specs.abstractions: ScalarQuantizer (per-dimension min/max), ProductQuantizer (M codebooks), SubspaceCodebook (256 centroids), ADCComputer (lookup table)
specs.concurrency_model: Quantizer training is single-threaded. ADC lookup table computation per query is stateless and parallelizable.

diagrams.title: Memory Usage: FP32 vs SQ8 vs PQ
diagrams.description: Bar chart comparing bytes per vector: FP32 (3072 for 768d), SQ8 (768), PQ-M8 (8). Shows compression ratios: 1x, 4x, 384x

diagrams.title: Scalar Quantization: Float32 → UInt8
diagrams.description: Shows min/max calibration, normalization to [0,1], scaling to [0,255], and reconstruction error bounds

diagrams.title: Product Quantization: Subspace Decomposition
diagrams.description: 768-dim vector split into 8 subvectors of 96 dims each, each quantized independently to 256 centroids

diagrams.title: PQ Codebook Training via K-Means
diagrams.description: Algorithm steps: extract subvectors → k-means++ init → assignment step → centroid update → repeat until convergence

diagrams.title: Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC)
diagrams.description: Shows query preprocessing (compute M×256 lookup table) and distance computation (M lookups + additions)

diagrams.title: ADC Lookup Table Structure
diagrams.description: 2D table: rows = M subspaces, columns = 256 centroid codes, cell = distance from query subvector to centroid

diagrams.title: Naive Decompression vs ADC Comparison
diagrams.description: Shows decompression overhead (M centroid lookups + concatenation) vs ADC (M table lookups only)

diagrams.title: HNSW+PQ: Two-Phase Search
diagrams.description: Phase 1: HNSW traversal with ADC distances → Phase 2: re-rank top candidates with exact distances

diagrams.title: SQ8 vs PQ Recall Comparison
diagrams.description: Curves showing recall@k: SQ8 (~0.92), PQ-M8 (~0.85), PQ-M16 (~0.90) vs FP32 ground truth

diagrams.title: Quantization Error Distribution
diagrams.description: Shows error per dimension for SQ8 (uniform ±0.004) and PQ (clustered near centroids)

modules.name: Query API & Server
modules.description: Exposes vector operations via REST API with concurrent access control, metadata filtering strategies, batch operations, and query timeouts. Handles collection lifecycle management and provides structured error responses.

specs.inputs: HTTP JSON requests (insert, search, delete, collection ops)
specs.outputs: HTTP JSON responses with results, errors, and latency metrics
specs.abstractions: Collection (config + storage + index), ThreadSafeCollection (RwLock wrapper), FilterPredicate (match, in, range, and, or, not), SearchRequest/SearchResponse
specs.concurrency_model: RwLock per collection. Multiple concurrent readers, single exclusive writer. Timeout checking during lock acquisition and iteration.
performance_targets: Batch insert 50%+ faster than N individual inserts
Zero data corruption under concurrent stress test (10 threads, 1000+ ops each)

implementation_phases.name: Batch insert with single lock acquisition
Batch search with shared storage access

diagrams.title: Query API Architecture Overview
diagrams.description: Layer diagram: HTTP → API handlers → ThreadSafeCollection → (Storage + Index + Metric) → Response

diagrams.title: Collection Lifecycle State Machine
diagrams.description: States: Creating → Active → Deleting → Deleted. Shows valid transitions and error states

diagrams.title: Concurrent Access: Read-Write Lock Model
diagrams.description: Shows multiple reader threads holding read locks simultaneously, writer thread blocked until all readers release

diagrams.title: Search Request Processing Flow
diagrams.description: Steps: validate dimension → acquire read lock → choose search mode → execute search → apply filter → update stats → return

diagrams.title: Pre-Filter vs Post-Filter for ANN
diagrams.description: Side-by-side: pre-filter (scan → filter → search filtered subset) vs post-filter (search → filter results → return)

diagrams.title: FilterStrategy::Auto Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Algorithm: estimate selectivity → if >30% use pre-filter else post-filter. Shows tradeoff reasoning

diagrams.title: Query Timeout Mechanism
diagrams.description: Shows deadline propagation: HTTP timeout → TimedSearch wrapper → periodic checks during iteration → early termination

diagrams.title: Batch Operation Efficiency
diagrams.description: Comparison: individual (N lock acquires, N validations) vs batch (1 lock acquire, N inserts, 1 unlock)

diagrams.title: REST API Endpoint Map
diagrams.description: Table: POST /collections, GET /collections, POST /collections/{id}/vectors, POST /collections/{id}/search, DELETE /collections/{id}

diagrams.title: Concurrent Stress Test Scenario
diagrams.description: Shows 5 reader threads + 5 writer threads operating simultaneously, barrier synchronization, error counting

diagrams.title: Error Response Structure
diagrams.description: JSON format: {"error": "error_type", "message": "details", "expected": X, "got": Y} for structured debugging

diagrams.title: FilterPredicate Evaluation Tree
diagrams.description: Shows AST structure for complex filters: And(Match(field, value), Or(Range(...), In(...)))

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Sequence: check CPUID for AVX2 → if supported, use avx2:: functions → else fall back to optimized scalar

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Brute-Force Linear Scan Flow
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Data flow: iterate all vectors → compute distance → update heap → extract sorted results. Shows O(N) distance computations

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Max-Heap Top-K Selection
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows heap structure: root = worst candidate in top-k, eviction when better candidate found, final extraction and sorting

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: O(N log k) vs O(N log N) Comparison
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Bar chart comparing operations: heap (3.3M comparisons for N=1M, k=10) vs sort (20M comparisons)

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Pre-Filtering vs Post-Filtering Tradeoff
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Decision flow: pre-filter saves computation but may not find k results; post-filter guarantees k but wastes work

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Batch Search Cache Efficiency
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows shared lock acquisition, vectors staying in L2/L3 cache across queries, sequential access pattern

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Ground Truth Export Format
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: JSON structure: metric_name, k, queries array with (query_vector, [(id, distance), ...]) pairs

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Recall@k Calculation
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Set intersection visualization: exact_ids ∩ approximate_ids / k. Shows recall = 0.8 example

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Brute-Force Scalability Cliff
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Latency curve: 10K (~5ms), 100K (~50ms), 1M (~500ms), 10M (~5s). Shows where brute-force becomes impractical

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Threshold-Based Search Flow
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Alternative to top-k: compute all distances → filter by threshold → return all within cutoff

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: FilterStrategy::Auto Decision Logic
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Algorithm: sample 100 vectors → estimate selectivity → if >50% pass use pre-filter else post-filter

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: HNSW Hierarchical Layer Structure
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: 3D visualization: Layer 2 (few nodes, long edges), Layer 1 (more nodes), Layer 0 (all nodes, short edges). Shows entry point at top

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Probabilistic Level Assignment Distribution
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Bar chart: ~50% at layer 0, ~25% at layer 1, ~12.5% at layer 2. Shows exponential decay from formula level = floor(-ln(uniform) * mL)

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Greedy Search with Layer Descent
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Sequence: start at entry point top layer → greedy move to closest neighbor → descend to next layer → repeat → base layer search with efSearch

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Two-Heap Search Structure
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows min-heap (candidates to explore) and max-heap (results, worst at top). Termination when closest candidate > worst result

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Node Insertion Process
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Steps: assign layer → find entry point → search each layer for neighbors → connect bidirectionally → prune if exceeding M_max

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Bidirectional Edge Maintenance
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows new node A connecting to neighbors B, C, D and their neighbor lists being updated to include A

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Neighbor Selection Heuristic
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Comparison: simple (take M closest) vs heuristic (prefer diversity, skip if closer to existing neighbor than to query)

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: efSearch vs Recall vs Latency Tradeoff
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Curves showing recall increases with efSearch (0.70→0.99) while latency also increases. Optimal region at efSearch=50-100

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: HNSW Index Serialization Format
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: JSON structure: config, nodes array (vector_id, max_layer, neighbors per layer), entry_point, max_layer, metric_name

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Recall@k Calculation Against Ground Truth
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows HNSW results compared to brute-force ground truth, intersection counting, recall formula

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: HNSW vs Brute-Force Latency Comparison
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Bar chart at different scales: 10K (similar), 100K (HNSW 10x faster), 1M (HNSW 50x faster)

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Skip List Analogy for Layer Assignment
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Side-by-side comparison of skip list levels and HNSW layers, showing identical probabilistic structure

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Memory Usage: FP32 vs SQ8 vs PQ
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Bar chart comparing bytes per vector: FP32 (3072 for 768d), SQ8 (768), PQ-M8 (8). Shows compression ratios: 1x, 4x, 384x

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Scalar Quantization: Float32 → UInt8
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows min/max calibration, normalization to [0,1], scaling to [0,255], and reconstruction error bounds

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Product Quantization: Subspace Decomposition
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: 768-dim vector split into 8 subvectors of 96 dims each, each quantized independently to 256 centroids

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: PQ Codebook Training via K-Means
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Algorithm steps: extract subvectors → k-means++ init → assignment step → centroid update → repeat until convergence

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC)
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows query preprocessing (compute M×256 lookup table) and distance computation (M lookups + additions)

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: ADC Lookup Table Structure
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: 2D table: rows = M subspaces, columns = 256 centroid codes, cell = distance from query subvector to centroid

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Naive Decompression vs ADC Comparison
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows decompression overhead (M centroid lookups + concatenation) vs ADC (M table lookups only)

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: HNSW+PQ: Two-Phase Search
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Phase 1: HNSW traversal with ADC distances → Phase 2: re-rank top candidates with exact distances

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: SQ8 vs PQ Recall Comparison
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Curves showing recall@k: SQ8 (~0.92), PQ-M8 (~0.85), PQ-M16 (~0.90) vs FP32 ground truth

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Quantization Error Distribution
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows error per dimension for SQ8 (uniform ±0.004) and PQ (clustered near centroids)

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Query API Architecture Overview
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Layer diagram: HTTP → API handlers → ThreadSafeCollection → (Storage + Index + Metric) → Response

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Collection Lifecycle State Machine
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: States: Creating → Active → Deleting → Deleted. Shows valid transitions and error states

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Concurrent Access: Read-Write Lock Model
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows multiple reader threads holding read locks simultaneously, writer thread blocked until all readers release

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Search Request Processing Flow
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Steps: validate dimension → acquire read lock → choose search mode → execute search → apply filter → update stats → return

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Pre-Filter vs Post-Filter for ANN
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Side-by-side: pre-filter (scan → filter → search filtered subset) vs post-filter (search → filter results → return)

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: FilterStrategy::Auto Decision Tree
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Algorithm: estimate selectivity → if >30% use pre-filter else post-filter. Shows tradeoff reasoning

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Query Timeout Mechanism
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows deadline propagation: HTTP timeout → TimedSearch wrapper → periodic checks during iteration → early termination

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Batch Operation Efficiency
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Comparison: individual (N lock acquires, N validations) vs batch (1 lock acquire, N inserts, 1 unlock)

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: REST API Endpoint Map
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Table: POST /collections, GET /collections, POST /collections/{id}/vectors, POST /collections/{id}/search, DELETE /collections/{id}

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Concurrent Stress Test Scenario
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows 5 reader threads + 5 writer threads operating simultaneously, barrier synchronization, error counting

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Error Response Structure
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: JSON format: {"error": "error_type", "message": "details", "expected": X, "got": Y} for structured debugging

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: FilterPredicate Evaluation Tree
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Shows AST structure for complex filters: And(Match(field, value), Or(Range(...), In(...)))
